content.assessment: No comparator country in the supplied evidence base has attempted capacity-cap or score-threshold contraction of a selective track sector as a standalone equity lever; the closest comparators (Finland, Poland) altered the age of first selection rather than shrinking place-counts within an unchanged tracking age. This limits direct transferability. The demographic-cover framing is comparatively untested in the evidence set—none of the cited international cases describe using cohort decline as political camouflage for contraction—so there is no outcome-relevant precedent to validate whether this approach achieves durable reduction in selectivity or merely produces sector-shifting, which is exactly the risk flagged for jurisdiction-limited state action (church/foundation schools absorbing displaced demand), a dynamic not documented in the German, Finnish, or Polish comparator cases, which operated within relatively unified public systems.
opportunities: Could draw indirectly on Finland's evidence that eliminating selective mechanisms (ability grouping abolition, 1985) had measurable if modest effects, suggesting capacity reduction paired with mechanism-level change might have more traction than capacity reduction alone
strengths: Avoids the single-year systemic shock that characterized Poland's 2017 reversal, since capacity contraction is not necessarily tied to an abrupt structural cutover
threats: Jurisdictional fragmentation (church/foundation schools outside central control) has no clean comparator precedent in the Finnish or Polish reforms, which operated in more unified public systems, so risk of selective demand migrating to an uncontrolled sector is a Hungary-specific transferability constraint not resolved by the cited comparator cases
weaknesses: No supplied comparator case demonstrates capacity-cap contraction of a selective track sector as an equity mechanism, so comparability with tested international approaches is minimal

The comparator cases show that reducing the age of tracking, not the sector size, is the lever associated with measured inequality reduction, meaning contraction targets a different variable than the one existing cross-national evidence links to outcomes
